sentences.text: OK.
Did you start already?
No.
Not yet.
OK, then I show you what I have to do.
My name is Chaskel-- Chaskel A.
Schlesinger.
Yes.
C-H-A-S-K-E-L-- A, Abrham, A-B-R-H-A-M-- Schlesinger, S-C-H-L-E-S-I-N-G-E-R.
No.
No.
September the 2nd, 1910.
I'll be 85 in September.
Wielopole.
Yes.
W-I-E-L-O-P-O-L-E.
Poland.
Before the war, in the last years before the war?
In childhood, I lived in this small town.
And my mother passed away when I was six years old.
And then it was a little bit disturbed in our house, because without our mother.
Then, six years later, my father passed away.
So I left-- after my father passed away, a year later, I left Wielopole for Tarnow.
I start-- had a little job somehow a few years.
And then I went in the business with two of my brothers together, in textile business.
We had a wholesale of textile, selling to clothing merchants-- manufacturers.
And that was going very well for us.
That was-- we started in 1929.
And in 1939, the war broke out.
It was a small town, a very-- like a family-- the whole town was like a family.
There was a marketplace.
And there was all around houses that were owned by Jews.
Very seldom that you had a Gentile living in town.
The Gentiles usually lived outside the town.
And it was-- we had a synagogue.
We had a besmedresh.
And it was a very, very quiet life.
No, I didn't go to school.
For the reason I didn't go to school was in 19-- when I was due for school in 1916, there was the First World War.
And all the school buildings and all the churches, everything was occupied by the armies, sometimes by our own armies, sometimes by the enemy armies, because it was going back and forth at that time.
And in 1919, when the war broke out, then came a little war between Poland and Russia.
And when Poland became a state in 1918, Russia didn't like it.
So they wanted to invade.
So there was another war between Russia and Poland, which took about a year and a half.
And at that time, one of my brothers, Moishe, was in the army.
And he was running away, because he didn't want to serve for the Polish army.
So that was a whole commotion.
And that was no time for me to school.
And in the 1923, when my father passed away, yeah, in the beginning, when they opened up the schools, there was no teachers.
There was a teacher that she might have-- maybe have finished her fourth grade.
She didn't know from anything.
And then was very anti-Semitic.
So Jews weren't allow to-- not they weren't allowed-- they were allowed to attend, but they insisted they should sit without caps.
And the Jewish people didn't want this.
So none of the Jewish people went to school.
I was taught-- my father hired a teacher.
So I was taught privately with another boy.
They brought in a teacher from another town, a Jewish teacher.
And he taught us.
And then in 1923, I went for Tarnow.
Yes.
I had-- we were nine originally.
But when the war broke out, we were only five.
One of my brothers was-- came back from the first army and he died, because he had a sickness from the army.
That was in 1917.
My mother passed away in 1916, and he passed away in 1917.
And another brother passed away in 1927.
And two brothers disappeared during the war by Hitler.
And one sister died a year before the war.
And her husband and seven children disappeared during the war by Hitler.
And my oldest brother had a wife and five children, and they all got killed by Hitler.
And my brother Moishe had a wife and three children.
The three children and his wife also disappeared, but he survived with us.
And another brother, Chaim, also was killed by Hitler.
Yeah, so we were five before the war.
I had-- you--
Before the war I had a grand-- one grandfather I remember where I was very little then.
He passed away.
And the other ones I never knew, because I was born after he died.
I was named after him.
My father was involved in a lot of things.
My father was a head of the Jewish community.
He was a gabbai in the synagogue.
He was a gabbai from the-- it was called the Sacred Society, for, you know, when people were dying.
It wasn't like here, that they had a chapel and things.
There were Jewish people that were involved in burials, so he was the gabbai of this society.
He was in the lumber business.
He used to buy wood-- forests, with a lot of partners.
Because there was big money involved in it.
So for instance, if he was-- he buy it, and he had some people chipping in money with a certain percentage.
That was-- unofficially, it was just done by vote.
They trusted each other.
And then he had people coming and chopping the woods.
And that was the system of heating for the wintertime.
We didn't have coals in this area.
And we didn't have any gas.
So that was the--
Right.
That was my father's business.
He was a wealthy-- he was very wealthy when he died.
Yeah, he had his own big-- built his own house, a two-story house at that time.
It was a big thing, and he was very wealthy.
No.
There was no organization at that time.
There was only the Jewish community in the town.
My days was-- mostly at time I went into the synagogue.
And I was have somebody teaching me something, like learning certain things.
That was spending the days.
Yeah, there were friends we went.
But there was no organizations, like in a small town like this, there was-- just got together in the backyards or wherever it was-- summertime more than wintertime.
That was the social thing.
My father-- six months later.
Because when he died, I was 12 and 1/2 years old.
So I waited for my bar mitzvah.
And after my bar mitzvah, I left.
My 13th birthday was September the 2nd.
And I left during the holidays, which was about three, four weeks after this.
No.
I went by myself.
I went by myself to Tarnow.
I had an uncle, which I went to him for the first few days.
And then I had a little job.
So I supported myself.
My brothers were still in Wielopole.
And then several years back, they came to Tarnow.
And when they came to Tarnow a few years later, we got into the business together.
I had a little experience from my jobs where I worked in a textile company.
So that's the way we started.
My business was-- at the beginning, from 1929 until 1934, it was a normal business-- quiet, hardly a living.
In 1934, we had our textile from Lodz.
You probably heard there was a textile manufacturing city in Lodz, in Poland, and also Bialystok.
That was the two major cities that they produced textile.
So in Lodz was a strike.
The working people got in a strike.
And during the strike, they bought everything, whatever could thing.
So our store was empty.
Because every day somebody came in and bought something, because you couldn't get any merchandise.
It was a shortage.
So we had empty shelves.
So I went to Lodz every three days to look for whatever I could get and send it home.
In the meantime, one manufacturer came and told me that the strike-- it was on a Friday-- the strike will be over on a Saturday.
And he has merchandise in the factory that can have in three days, I should buy.
So I bought it, a big amount.
And I gave him a big amount of money to his down payment.
And he gave my receipt.
And two days later, the strike wasn't over.
It was going on another few weeks.
In the meantime, the merchandise went up, much higher.
And he had a commitment to give us things.
Then the big push came over that we made a lot of money this summer.
And then they start going better, and better, and better.
So that was the result.
And then we bought a lot of things in Bialystok.
And then we had a piece of partnership in Bialystok, which during the war, we left everything in-- You had a partnership in a factory there?
Yeah, we had-- we had agreement with a manufacturer that for Tarnow he cannot sell to anybody, only to us.
In the meantime, they went bankrupt.
And when he went bankrupt, he couldn't have-- couldn't things.
And we didn't want to lose him.
So we gave him money to stay in business, and we were partners-- unofficially, again.
Actually, it was officially.
But it wasn't on the name, for the registered as partners.
In the meantime, that was in 1936, '37, '38.
And in '39, the war broke out and everything was left there.
So I never went back there.
So what were the first signs to you of any danger, anything?
During the war, you mean?
Yes, the first time-- the Germans came in on the fifth day of the war.
And I went with my brother Chaim and my older brother, Moishe, lived in a very fancy street.
When the army came in, it was a little bit quiet in the afternoon, I says to my brother, let's go.
There was next building to where my brother lived was a wholesale from chocolate.
I says, it's good to have something in the house in case we don't know-- sometimes you cannot get any bread or something.
Let's go and buy.
In the meantime, when I came to this building, I saw the army, some soldiers, run into my brother's house.
I says to my brother, let's go and see Moishe's house.
So we came there, and all of  a sudden they took us, and they put us in front of the wall with the hands up.
And my brother took a nap in the house.
So they came up, and they-- my sister-in-law was holding the baby in her arm.
And they told him to come down.
So he came down.
He didn't have his shoes on, because he was napping.
And they put him also like this.
Whoever was in this building.
What happened was in the back of this building was a church.
And somebody shoot from the church to the army.
So they came, ran all around to see.
And in the meantime, when-- the people that went to the other side, in the church, found some Polish people there, what they were shooting to the Germans.
So they took them out.
And at that time, they let us go, but it didn't-- it wasn't too much of a-- think that we would be shot, all three of us.
So this was one time.
The next time was when they start grabbing people on the street to clean up the war-- the messes.
For instance, they came into the park.
And they parked their trucks in the park, and they start washing themselves and eating and threw away can with the things.
So they kept Jewish people on the street to clean up.
So I was between them, because I didn't have-- I didn't have any patience to sit in the house, because I was single and young.
So I was always on the street.
And that's why I was always in danger.
So they took me.
But at that time, it was not so bad, because they grabbed you for a few hours, then they let you go.
But then started, again, with trucks that they took people away from town.
And it took a week or 10 days until you found out where they-- from the relatives, where they have.
So that was a camp not too far from town, which was called Pskov.
They grabbed a lot of people in the street.
I was once almost kept, but I ran away.
So I missed these things.
That was, again, same thing.
And that was going on for two years like this.
They grabbed people, and they took them away, and they didn't know.
And two years later, then they started officially the deportations.
That was in 1942.
That was the 10th of June, 1942.
They ordered every Jew to be registered.
So I went to register.
And I had a [INAUDIBLE] card, which was called a working paper.
And I had a Judenpass.
And in the morning, when I heard registration, I had a neighbor, a young doctor, which I was friendly with him.
He went with the Judenrat.
First they ordered the Judenrat to come to register with the doctors.
So when he came back, he showed me.
I says, what do you have on this paper?
So he showed me he had a seal, a big seal, with the Gestapo.
And then, when they ordered us to go to it, I was-- the lines were long, for miles.
And it was a very hot day.
Finally, I came into these things.
And I had my papers was-- in the beginning, I had the papers as a [INAUDIBLE], which means businessman.
And I realized that they don't need businesspeople.
They need working people.
So I went to a locksmith.
And I paid him off that he should seal my papers that I worked for him as a locksmith.
And that's why I am registered on Schindler's list as a locksmith.
And when I had this paper, I came to register, he had this little seal in his hand.
And he wanted to put the little seal, which was a K, that meant probably concentration camp.
So I says, no, that I'm not a businessman anymore.
I am a locksmith.
So he changed the seal.
And he put a big seal on this, which I was very happy.
And then I pushed in my Judenpass, which I didn't need it.
I don't know, for some reason I did this.
And it came out handy very-- handy later.
So he put this big seal on this paper too.

When I came home, the next day, during the night, the Judenrat was ordered all the Jewish ordnungsdienst, you know, the Jewish police people, to come to go around with the list to tell people to come out to the deportation.
A lot of people didn't want to come.
So they were in hiding.
So the SS were very wild for this, and they start grabbing people, regardless, whatever the seal was, regardless.
They didn't honor anything.
And they grabbed people.
And they shot at the same day 10,000 people in the city and about 8,000 people they deported.
That was the 11th of June.
And that was called the first deportation.
And that was taking place three days.
The Thursday the 11th, Monday the 15th, and the next Thursday the 18th-- all three days was called first deportation.
On the second day, I knew Margot at that time.
We were friendly, but we weren't tied in any way.
So I went up to their house before, during the registration, and I told them to go and hide-- her and her parents.
So her father didn't want hear from it.
He was an older man, and he didn't want to go to hide.
So they told Margot she should go.
So she went someplace outside of the city, and she was hiding.
And during this, after Monday-- she was there during Monday, the 15th.
On Tuesday, I went to the Judenrat.
And I made out a [INAUDIBLE], which is a marriage-- not a marriage license-- a paper that we are married.
I paid off in the Judenrat.
And they gave me this paper.
But I ask him to go to the Gestapo.
So I didn't want to go to the Gestapo.
So I had already, I sent somebody with the paper, to Margot, where she was outside of the city.
And when she came back, she saw her apartment where her parents were was sealed.
She couldn't go in anymore there.
So she went to-- she came to my house where I lived with my brother, Chaim.
And she had this paper.
So I gave her my Judenpass.
And I says, if they come, you show it.
And in the meantime, my brother had also this big seal.
So when they came to the house, they never opened up the door or never knocked on the door.
They break the door.
They kick the door and break.
When I heard them coming at the stairs, so I opened up the door, so I didn't want to break the door.
So they came in, and they says ausweis, which is identification.
So I showed them mine.
And he gave it back to me.
They came in-- first of all, the one came in with a cane and with a machine gun.
And the other ones were looking all over every corner and under the bed.
And he said to my brother-- my brother showed him his paper.
And my wife showed him this paper from the marriage.
And he says, that's nothing.
You come.
So I says Margot, why don't you show my Judenpass-- the Judenpass.
It was my Judenpass, my name, my thing.
So she showed it to him.
He says, oh, that's OK.
So they left.
So she was safe at that time.
On the third day, on the last day, we didn't trust.
So we were hiding.
We were hiding someplace, all three of us, and-- and now, the third day, no-- they said that they going to honor-- whoever has the big seal will be honored.
So we stayed in the house.
And that was June-- after the 18th of June.
Then a day later or two days later, they assigned an area where the ghettos should be.
So that wasn't close, the ghetto.
That was assigned, the ghetto.
So I have to leave my apartment, because my apartment was outside of the area.
But Margot's parents' apartment was in the ghetto.
So we went there, and they opened up the apartment, and we moved in there.
So we moved in there-- my brother and I and Margot.
And then we took in another four people, because the Judenrat forced everybody to move in together-- some rooms for 8 or 10 people in one room.
We had a kitchen in our room.
So we were seven people at that time.
So we moved in there.
And a few days later, somebody comes up and asks me-- I never-- that was a friend of mine.
I never saw him during the war time, because he left right after-- right when the war broke out, he left Tarnow.
I didn't know from him.
Then he came up.
And he says to me he worked for Madritsch, was a company by the name Madritsch in Krakow.
He made uniform for the Germans and also civil clothes.
So he says, Margot, Krakow didn't have too many tailors.
If I be willing organize, Madritsch wants to open up a branch in Tarnow.
So I says, yes.
So he went back.
He says, OK.
And two days later, Madritsch came, and I met him.
And he brought me a pass that I can go in the area from the ghetto and out.
And he took over a building there.
And the building-- it was an empty building-- a Jewish building, actually.
And he had an office-- four rooms.
And he had put in a telephone.
And he said I should be there every day.
So I was there.
And a few days later-- in the meantime, I spread the rumors in the ghetto that Madritsch wants to open up.
Everybody was anxious to work, because they knew that's the only way they can be saved, if they work for a German company.
So everybody came with their machines.
And a few days later, Madritsch sent in work, and we worked for Madritsch from August 1942 until September 1943.
In the meantime, we walked out from the ghetto every day, under the Judenrat police, in the ghetto and out of the ghetto, because the working place was outside of the ghetto.
And we used to buy something.
We didn't use the kitchen, but basically I had a kitchen there that we used to get to food from the kitchen.
But we had still money.
We didn't want to use the kitchen food from them.
So we also-- and the Polish people came around to this building all the time.
And they handed in some food.
And they would throw them out the back with the money.
So we had a man assigned to stay by the door, not to-- to [INAUDIBLE] not to let the Gentile people in.
If somebody wants to buy something, so we ask him to relieve.
He should go someplace and we stayed.
So one day, I was there for 15 minutes to buy something.
And I just had handed in from a woman some eggs, some butter or something.
And put it-- we had a hiding place over there.
I threw out the bag with the money.
In the same minute came a Gestapo, which was a real [INAUDIBLE].
His name was Grunow.
And all of a sudden, I see his gun near my head.
And he says, was machst du hier?
This means what are you doing here?
And I says, I'm watching that the Gentile people shouldn't come in here.
So somehow-- he had a tailor in the same building-- on the second floor was his private tailor.
So he went up to the tailor.
And in the meantime, I called back this same guy that he's supposed to stay on the door.
And I run away.
I didn't want to stay there anymore because I was afraid.
That was one time that I was saved.
Then, a few days later-- or a week later, a few weeks later-- we had another incident.
We-- like a woman bought some butter.
And she brought it into the ghetto.
While we're walking in the ghetto, the Gestapo was there and including this Grunow.
And he-- the whole group-- the men were walking first, in fifth rows, like the army, and the women in the back.
He ordered that we should stay.
So we stayed there.
And all of the sudden, I see something is flying over the fence-- a package.
So he came-- and he noticed too-- so he came there.
And he schlepped-- my wife had a raincoat with a hat.
He pulls her out.
And I saw from far-- I didn't know exactly that's her, but I saw that he grabbed somebody.
And I knew that's the area where Margot was standing there.
So in the meantime, he realized, and he didn't take her.
He the other one and shot her.
Because she threw it over-- a butter-- a pound of butter over the fence.
She figured maybe after this he goes out.
She's going to get it back.
So that was another incident.
Do you remember what you were thinking at that time?
Huh?
I was thinking-- I was-- in the beginning, I was almost sure that's Margot.
And then when I saw that they took her, pulled her out and tossed the fence, so I saw that's not Margot.
And then-- then came the high holidays.
They always managed during the high holidays to make some incidents.
Yeah, and before things in November of 1939, they start burning the synagogues.
On one Sunday, all of the sudden-- we were sitting in the house, and all of the sudden we see fire all over.
So we start looking out, and there was every synagogue in the city was burnt.
It was nighttime.
The city was light up like daylight.
It was so light.
And they burnt the synagogue.
That was in November 1939 still.
I jumped over to '42.
